hints: Treat each collision as the two ants passing through each other.
Then every left-moving ant passes every right-moving ant exactly once.
solution: Treating collisions as pass-throughs, each of Alice's $40$ ants crosses each of Bob's $80$ ants exactly once, so there are $40\times80=3200$ collisions.

hints: P(all pieces > a) = (1−3a)² for two cuts.
solution: For two uniform cuts, the probability all three pieces exceed $a$ is $(1-3a)^2$. With $a=0.05$: $(1-0.15)^2=0.7225$, so $P(\text{shortest}\le0.05)=1-0.7225=0.2775=\dfrac{111}{400}.$

hints: Correlation is scale-invariant; signs of the slopes flip it.
solution: Correlation is invariant to positive scaling and flips sign with a negative slope. The slopes $+2$ and $-4$ give a sign of $-1$, so $\rho_{Y_1,Y_2}=-0.2.$

hints: Re-rolling doesn't change relative odds; condition on a decisive roll.
solution: Conditioning on a decisive roll, double-$6$ has probability $\tfrac1{11}$ and a $6$-with-non-$6$ has $\tfrac{10}{11}$. The game is fair when $\tfrac1{11}(100)-\tfrac{10}{11}x\ge0$, i.e. $x\le10$. So the maximum is $x=10.$

hints: For each first roll k, compute the reroll value (pay k if sum≥7, else get the 2nd roll).
Reroll beats keeping only for k=1 (value 7/3); keep 2,3,4,5,6.
solution: For first roll $k$, the reroll's expected value is $\tfrac16\sum_j[\,j\text{ if }k+j<7\text{ else }-k\,]$, which only beats keeping when $k=1$ (worth $\tfrac73$). So the optimal payoff per first roll is $\tfrac73,2,3,4,5,6$, giving $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac16\big(\tfrac73+20\big)=\tfrac{67}{18}.$

hints: The error e_n=G_n−F_n satisfies the same Fibonacci recurrence with e_100=1, e_99=0.
Each weighing gives 3 outcomes, so k weighings distinguish 3^k cases.
solution: Each weighing has $3$ outcomes, distinguishing at most $3^k$ cases, and there are $90\times2=180$ possibilities. Since $3^4=81<180\le243=3^5$, the minimum is $5$ weighings.

hints: First return of a ±1 walk to 0 at step 2n has probability (1/(2n−1))C(2n,n)(pq)ⁿ.
solution: The first time heads and tails are equal corresponds to a $\pm1$ walk's first return to $0$, which occurs at step $2n$ with probability $\dfrac{1}{2n-1}\binom{2n}{n}(pq)^n$. For $p=\tfrac13,n=5$: $\dfrac{1}{9}\binom{10}{5}(\tfrac29)^5=\dfrac{252}{9}\cdot\dfrac{32}{59049}\approx0.0152.$

hints: All three odds before any even ⟺ the three odds are the first three values to appear among all six.
First-appearance order of the six faces is uniformly random.
solution: We need all three odd faces to appear before any even face — i.e. the three odds occupy the first three positions in the (uniformly random) order of first appearances of the six faces. That probability is $\dfrac{3!\,3!}{6!}=\dfrac{36}{720}=\dfrac1{20}.$

hints: Each element has 3 options: exclude, include, or bold-include.
solution: Each of the $6$ elements is independently excluded, plainly included, or bold-included — $3$ choices each — giving $3^6=729$ sets.

hints: R²=1−SSE/SST; duplicating data doubles both SSE and SST.
solution: Duplicating each point doubles both $\text{SSE}$ and $\text{SST}$, leaving $R^2=1-\tfrac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$ unchanged at $0.56.$

hints: Optimal play clusters all 'place' actions first, then all 'take' actions.
After p places, the two boxes hold random amounts summing to p; t=100−p takes empty random boxes (re-taking an empty box yields 0).
Write expected payoff as a function of p and maximize.
